title: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: immune-evading capsids, re-targeted serotypes, and next-gen oncolytics

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: immune-evading capsids, re-targeted serotypes, and next-gen oncolytics
We come, in our last segment, to the frontier.
Where is adenoviral vector engineering going in the next ten years?
Let me sketch the main directions.
Immune-evading capsids.
The pre-existing immunity problem and the acute innate response are still the central limitations of adenoviral vectors.
Several groups are working on capsids engineered to be invisible to common neutralizing antibodies.

One approach: replace immunodominant epitopes on the hexon surface with sequences from rare or non-human serotypes, producing a chimeric hexon that retains the structural function of the original but is not recognized by antibodies against the parent serotype.
Another approach: directed evolution of capsids in the presence of human serum, selecting for variants that escape neutralization.
Another: chemical modification (PEGylation and beyond) to physically shield the antigenic surface.

None of these has yet produced a fully invisible vector, but the field is making steady progress, and a vector that could be effectively redosed without neutralization would be transformative — it would unlock multi-dose gene therapy regimens that are currently impossible.
Re-targeted serotypes for new tissues.
Specific tissues remain hard to target with current vectors — the central nervous system from the bloodstream is the canonical example, because of the blood-brain barrier.
New capsid engineering is exploring vectors that can cross the blood-brain barrier or target specific cell populations within the brain.
Similar work is happening on muscle, on hematopoietic stem cells, on specific tumor populations.

The combination of natural serotype mining (looking at rare human and animal adenoviruses for novel tropisms) and engineered re-targeting (modifying fibers and other capsid components) is expanding the menu.
Next-generation oncolytics.
Oncolytic adenoviruses with multiple armaments — combinations of immune-stimulating cytokines, checkpoint inhibitor payloads, bispecific T cell engagers — are moving through clinical trials.
The vision is a tumor-targeted virus that not only kills cancer cells directly but also recruits and activates the patient's own immune system to attack the tumor systemically.
Combinations of oncolytic adenovirus with systemic checkpoint inhibitor antibodies (which we discussed in the antibody lecture) are showing meaningful clinical activity in melanoma and other tumors.
New vaccine targets.

Following the COVID success, adenoviral vaccines are being developed against many other pathogens — HIV (a major historical target, with mixed clinical results so far), tuberculosis, malaria, RSV, multiple hemorrhagic fever viruses.
ChAdOx1 and Ad26 are both being deployed across multiple programs simultaneously.

The challenge for the next generation of vaccines will be to maintain efficacy with repeated use across pathogens — patients who get an Ad26-based COVID vaccine now have anti-Ad26 antibodies that may interfere with future Ad26-based vaccines for other diseases.
The vector immunology that constrains gene therapy now constrains vaccine deployment too, and engineering around it will require continued serotype diversification.
Manufacturing innovation.
Continuous bioprocessing, single-use bioreactors, intensified upstream processes — the manufacturing technology continues to evolve toward higher productivity and lower cost.
Production costs of a few cents per vaccine dose are achievable at scale, which is what made adenoviral vaccines so important for low-income countries during COVID.
Further cost reductions could open up vaccine deployment for diseases that are currently economically marginal.
Combination platforms.

The future may involve adenoviral vectors used in combination with other platforms — adenoviral prime followed by mRNA boost, or adenoviral delivery combined with protein subunit boosters, or even multiple adenoviral serotypes used in sequence.
The mix-and-match studies done during COVID showed that combining platforms can produce broader and more durable responses than any single platform alone.
Let me close with the arc one more time, because the arc is what I want you to leave with.
Adenovirus was discovered in 1953 as a respiratory pathogen.
It was engineered into a vector in the late 1980s and early 1990s.
It became the great hope of gene therapy in the mid-1990s.
It became the great tragedy of gene therapy in 1999, with Jesse Gelsinger's death.
It was redesigned across three generations of engineering through the 2000s.
It found new life as a vaccine platform in the 2010s, with chimpanzee and rare-serotype vectors.
And it was deployed at planetary scale in 2020 and 2021, with hundreds of millions of people vaccinated against COVID-19 using adenoviral vector vaccines.
The same virus, the same fundamental biology, played all those roles.
The truck was the same truck.
We just kept learning how to use it.

If you remember nothing else, remember this: the immune response to adenovirus is the central feature of the platform, and the history of adenoviral vectors is the history of figuring out which jobs to use it for and which to avoid.
For gene therapy in tissues where you need quiet, sustained expression — pick AAV.
For vaccines, where you want a strong immune response to a delivered antigen — adenovirus shines.
For cancer therapy, where you want immune recruitment to a tumor — oncolytic adenovirus may be the right tool.
The vector hasn't changed.
Our understanding of where to deploy it has.
And remember Jesse Gelsinger.
The field that built the COVID vaccines is the same field that asked a healthy eighteen-year-old to volunteer for a Phase I trial that killed him.
The lessons learned from his death — about dose, about route, about disclosure, about humility — are baked into the design of every vector trial that runs today.
He didn't volunteer in vain.
The infrastructure of clinical responsibility that grew up after 1999 is part of why we could deploy hundreds of millions of doses of adenoviral vaccines two decades later without catastrophe.
The tragedy and the comeback are connected.
That's the lecture.
Thanks for sticking with me through two hours of dense material.
Take a break, talk to each other, and bring me your questions next time.
We're done.
